summary:
The domestication of the common bean (Phaseolus vulgaris) and other bean species in Mesoamerica and South America occurred in parallel, with evidence from multiple regions pointing to a timeframe of approximately 7000–6000 BCE. Wild progenitors of the common bean grew across a wide range in the Americas, and multiple independent domestication events appear to have taken place in highland Mexico and the Andes.

Beans formed a nutritional cornerstone of early American agriculture. Their high protein content and complementarity with maize — together providing a near-complete amino acid profile — made them fundamental to the development of sedentary agricultural societies throughout the Americas. The combination of maize, beans, and squash, later known as the Three Sisters in Mesoamerican and North American traditions, represented one of the most nutritionally balanced and agriculturally efficient cropping systems developed anywhere in the ancient world.

read_more_links.title: Domestication of Plants in the Americas – Britannica
